segments.s089-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; بشنید is a simple past verb 'he heard' with نوذر as its subject (proper name). که introduces the reported content (that-clause) and قارن برفت is that content: قارن is a proper name here (written as a common noun in the lexicon) and برفت is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'went/left' (بر + رفت = 'went away'). The whole segment is a temporal subordinate clause ('When Nowzar heard that Qāren had gone...').
segments.s089-g2: دمان از پسش روی بنهاد و تفت is a coordinated phrase describing Nowzar's immediate reaction: دمان (lit. 'hem/skirt') is used idiomatically to mean 'in pursuit/at the brink' (here 'closely after him'); از پسش = 'from behind him/after him' marks direction; روی بنهاد is a compound verb (روی 'face' + بنهاد, past of نهادن) meaning 'he set his face/tipped himself toward' = 'turned toward' or 'set off after'; و تفت adds a brief verb describing the manner ('and hastened'/'and rushed') — تفت is a terse, poetic verb of motion/intensity here. Word order is subject omitted (understood Nowzar) + directional phrase + verb + coordinating verb.
segments.s089-g3: همی تاخت is a durative past construction: همی (ongoing/still) + تاخت (past of تاختن, 'he galloped/charged'), so 'he kept rushing'. کز introduces a subordinate clause of purpose or result ('that/so that'), روز بد = 'an unlucky/bad day', and بگذرد is a present/subjunctive-like form 'may pass' or 'should pass' (the verb گذر/بگذرد used here to express hoped-for passing of the bad day).
segments.s089-g4: سپهرش = 'his sky/heaven' (possessive suffix -ش). مگر here functions as 'lest'/'unless' introducing the feared consequence; زیر پی = 'under the foot' (زیر 'under' + پی 'foot/step' here), and نسپرد is negative/optative of سپردن 'to hand over/entrust' (here 'be handed over/consigned'). The clause therefore expresses the fear that his fate/heaven might be consigned under someone's feet. Word order: possessee + linking particle + negated verb of handing-over as the consequence clause.
segments.s089-g5: چو 'when' introduces the clause; افراسیاب is the subject (proper name). آگهی یافت is the light-verb expression 'obtained news/learned information' (آگهی = 'news/information', یافت = 'found/obtained'), and زوی = 'from him' (ز + وی). The construction means 'When Afrasiab received word from him...'.
segments.s089-g6: که introduces the reported content: سوی بیابان = 'toward the desert' (سوی = toward, بیابان = desert). نهادست is a classical/resultant perfect-like form of نهادن (نهاد + است) meaning 'has set/has placed' or here 'has turned/has set (his face)' and روی = 'face' (object of نهادن in the idiom روی نهادن). So the clause means 'that he had set his face toward the desert.' The verb form نهادست is archaic/poetic (stative/resultative).
segments.s089-g7: سپاه انجمن کرد is a light-verb noun phrase: سپاه 'army' + انجمن 'assembly' + کرد 'made/did' = 'he assembled an army' (کرد is the main verb). و is the coordinator 'and'. پویان برفت uses پویان (participle/adjective 'the active/moving ones' = 'pursuers') as the subject of برفت ('went off/left'); برفت is the archaic/poetic past 'went away'. Overall this segment reports that the army was gathered and the pursuers departed.
segments.s089-g8: چو شیر 'like a lion' (چو as a simile 'as/like', شیر 'lion'). از پسش = 'after him/from his rear' (direction), روی بنهاد is the same verbal idiom 'he set/turned his face' (بنهاد = past of نهادن), i.e. 'he turned to pursue', and و تفت again adds the manner 'and rushed/charged' — the final verb tenses are concise, poetic pasts; the subject is implied from the previous clause. The sequence is simile + directional phrase + result/action.
segments.s089-g25: همو is an emphatic pronoun 'that same one / he' placed before the objects for emphasis. تاج و تخت is a coordinated noun phrase ('crown and throne'); بلندی (literally 'height/loftiness') functions as a modifier qualifying تخت (i.e. 'a lofty throne') and appears after the nouns in typical Persian adjective/attribute position. دهد is third-person singular (classical/present-style narrative form) meaning 'gives/bestows' and comes at the end of the clause.
segments.s089-g26: The clause repeats the subject/emphatic pronoun همو ('that same one') to contrast the two sorts of gifts; تیرگی and نژندی are coordinated direct objects ('gloom/darkness' and 'disgrace/humiliation'). دهد again is the third-person singular verbal form 'gives/causes' — here the nuance can be 'inflicts' as well as 'gives', fitting the negative pair.
segments.s089-g27: به marks the target/recipient ('to/for the enemy'); the verb ماند ('remains') appears with the archaic/habitual particle همی before it (همی ماند) to indicate ongoing or characteristic action. There is no explicit subject here (it is carried over from the previous clause), and the repeated به ... به ... structure lists two targets: دشمن and دوست.
segments.s089-g28: گهی ... گاه sets up a parallel 'sometimes ... at other times ...' structure. مغز ('core/essence') is the object placed before the verb یابی ('you/one find' — present/generic 'finds/obtain'), and ازو ('from him/of him') is a postpositional genitive indicating source. The second half repeats the adverbial گاه and a contrasting object پوست ('skin', i.e. mere surface). The clause is generic: 'sometimes one finds the essence, sometimes only the skin.'
segments.s089-g29: سرت‌گر is the fused conditional phrase (سرت + گر) meaning 'if upon your head...' where گر = 'if'. بساید here is a classical subjunctive/jussive verb meaning 'may sit/settle' or 'falls (upon)' (poetic usage), and به ابر سیاه is the prepositional complement 'on/against a black/dark cloud.' The clause expresses a conditional situation ('if a black cloud settles over your head').
segments.s089-g30: سرانجام is an adverb 'in the end / finally.' خاک is the predicate noun 'dust/earth' and است is the copula 'is.' ازو ('of him/from him') marks whose place it is (postnominal genitive), and جایگاه is 'place/standing.' The literal order 'سرانجام خاک است ازو جایگاه' = 'in the end, dust is his place' → 'his final resting place is the dust.'
segments.s089-g31: وزان پس is a temporal connective meaning 'and from that / after that.' بفرمود is the honorific/polite past tense form meaning 'he said' or 'he commanded' (the prefix ب‑ appears in classical narrative forms), and the proper name افراسیاب follows the verb — an example of poetic inversion where the verb precedes its subject.
segments.s089-g32: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'saying that'). از is the preposition 'from' and it governs a simple coordinated list (غار و کوه و بیابان و آب = 'cave and mountain and wilderness and water'), each item linked by و. The clause is the beginning of reported speech or a list of origins/places.
segments.s089-g17: A nominal phrase listing the affected people: 'خود' (= himself/one's self) followed by 'و' (and) and 'نامداران' (the renowned men) with the numeral 'هزار و دویست' (one thousand and two hundred) quantifying them. In Classical Persian the numeral commonly follows the noun it counts.
segments.s089-g18: 'تو گفتی' is a reporting clause ('you said'). The following clause 'کشان بر زمین جای نیست' is reported content: 'کشان' is a verbal adjective/participle meaning 'dragging' or 'to be dragged,' 'بر زمین' is the locative phrase 'on the ground,' and 'جای نیست' is an existential construction 'there is no place.' Word order ends with the negated copular verb 'نیست.'
segments.s089-g19: 'بسی راه جستند' is a past-tense clause: 'بسی' (many/much) modifies 'راه' (ways/routes) and 'جستند' is past plural of 'جستن' used here in the sense 'to seek/try' (i.e., 'they searched for/attempted many paths'). 'و بگریختند' is a coordinate past clause 'and they fled.'
segments.s089-g20: 'به دام بلا' is a prepositional phrase 'into the snare/trap of calamity' (به = into/towards, دام = trap, بلا = calamity). 'هم' links this clause to the previous one ('also/likewise'), and 'برآویختند' is past plural meaning 'they fell upon/into' (a sudden falling into the trap).
segments.s089-g21: 'چنان' ('thus/such') introduces the manner; 'لشکری را' (the army, definite by object marker 'را') is the object. 'گرفته' is a past participle 'having taken/seized,' and 'به بند' ('into bonds') states the condition (i.e., taken captive). Together this forms a participial clause that modifies the main verb that follows.
segments.s089-g22: 'بیاورد' is the main verb (he brought). 'با شهریار بلند' is a prepositional/phrase indicating the addressee or presence: 'با' here marks accompaniment or presentation 'before/with' the 'شهریار' (king), qualified by 'بلند' (lofty/high). In narrative Persian 'بیاورد' is used for the past action 'he brought.'
segments.s089-g23: 'اگر' introduces a conditional ('if'). 'با تو گردون نشیند' literally 'the heavens/fate sit with you' — an idiom meaning 'fortune favors you'; 'نشیند' is a third-person verb ('sits') here used in a subjunctive/optative-tinged conditional sense. 'به راز' ('in secret') uses 'به' as an adverbial/forming phrase 'in/by (secret).'
segments.s089-g24: 'هم' ('also/still') connects the result; 'از گردش او' literally 'from his/its turning/cycle' (i.e., from the revolutions/changes of fortune). 'نیابی' is the negative present/optative form 'you will not/find not' (ن + یابی), meaning 'you will not obtain/fnd,' and 'جواز' here means 'justification/authorization' (i.e., no excuse or justification will be found in those turns).
segments.s089-g41: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the speech-verb to introduce reported speech. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (third-person); با + personal name (با ویسه) here marks the addressee ('to Viseh') — in older/poetic Persian با can indicate the person spoken to rather than a literal accompaniment. نامور is an epithet after the name: an adjective 'renowned' describing ویسه.
segments.s089-g42: که introduces the content of the speech ('that' / 'that he said…' — here functioning as a complementizer). دل‌سخت is an adjectival phrase 'hard‑hearted' or 'resolute'. گردان here is a verbal-form (classical/poetic usage) meaning 'make/turn' (imperative/jussive sense: 'make [your heart] hard'); به marks the target/direction ('toward, with regard to'), and مرگ پسر means 'the son's death' (مرگ = death; پسر = son). The clause gives an order/content of what was said: 'make your heart hard toward the son's death.'
segments.s089-g43: که چون at the start functions as a temporal conjunction ('when') introducing the circumstance. قارن is the agent/name (Qaran) placed before the objects in the poetic word order. کاوه is the person brought into the fight (Kaveh). جنگ آورد is a light-verb construction: آورد (past 'brought') takes جنگ as the activity — 'brought (him) to battle' or 'led (him) into combat.'
segments.s089-g44: پلنگ is a sobriquet/epithet 'the panther' used as the subject. از شتابش is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of his haste' (از = 'from/because of'; شتاب + ـش = 'his haste'). درنگ آورد is a compound meaning 'caused a pause' or more simply 'paused' (درنگ = pause; آورد used causatively here = 'brought about a pause' → 'paused/checked').
segments.s089-g45: ترا را is the object phrase 'you' (ت = clitic for تو, and را = object/contrast marker); here ترا را is fronted in word order. رفت is the verbal root 'go' (here part of a periphrastic necessity: 'you must go'). باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary'. ببسته کمر is a verbal adjective/compound meaning 'having girded the waist' or an instruction 'gird your loins' (ببسته = 'tied/girt' used in classical/poetic style; کمر = waist / belt). Together the segment expresses necessity plus the preparatory action: 'you must go — gird your waist.'
segments.s089-g46: یکی here functions as 'one/a single' or 'a certain' (indefinite) modifying لشکری — 'an army.' لشکری is an adjectival/noun form meaning 'army / troops.' ساخته is a participial/verb form 'made/formed' (in poetry it carries the sense of 'raise/assemble'), and پرهنر is an adjectival epithet 'skilled' or 'well‑trained' describing the army. The phrase commands assembling a single skilled army: 'make/raise a skilful army.'
segments.s089-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. تنگ here functions as a noun meaning 'distress/straiteness' (poetic usage) and اندر آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'came in / fell upon' (اندر = 'in/within', آمد = past 'came'); بر شهریار is the prepositional phrase 'upon the king' (preposition بر + noun شهریار). Word order is somewhat inverted/poetic but literal: 'when [distress] came in upon the king.'
segments.s089-g10: همش here means essentially 'all of it / nothing but' modifying the following infinitive. تاختن is the infinitive/masdar 'to charge/ride against', and دید is the past-tense verb 'he saw' taking that infinitive as its object: 'he saw charging' = 'he saw nothing but charging'. و هم کارزار links the second noun phrase with و 'and' plus هم 'also' meaning 'and also battle/fighting' — a compact poetic coordination.
segments.s089-g11: بدان سان is a fixed poetic phrase 'in that manner / thus' (ب + دان + سان combined). که introduces the subordinate/result clause. آمد is the past 'he came', and همی جست is a habitual/iterative past ('همی' is an archaic continuous/adverbial marker meaning 'kept / repeatedly', and جست is past 'leapt, sprang'); راه is the object 'the road/way'. The clause therefore reads: 'in that way he came, repeatedly springing along the road.'
segments.s089-g12: که تا introduces a purposive/result clause 'so that / until'. تا here governs a following verb expressing purpose/result. بر سر آرد is the verb phrase with آرد (3rd-singular present/subjunctive of آوردن) meaning 'bring/put upon' (بر + سر = 'on the head'); سری is 'a head' (object) and بی‌کلاه is an adjectival phrase 'without a helmet'. Grammatically, آرد appears as the expected verb after تا to express the intended/resultant action: 'so that he might bring a head un‑helmeted onto the (victim's) head.'
segments.s089-g13: شب تیره is a noun + adjective 'the dark night'. تا شد is a temporal construction 'until it became' (تا = until, شد = became). بلند آفتاب is a poetic inversion meaning 'the sun high' (literally 'high sun'), so together: 'the dark night lasted until the sun became high' = 'until the sun rose high.'
segments.s089-g14: همی is the archaic continuous/adverbial particle 'then/thereupon' (or 'still/indeed' depending on context). گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned / came to be'; با is the preposition 'with / upon' and links the following names (نوذر افراسیاب). In this context گشت با + names means 'the day/sun came upon/affected Nowzar and Afrasiab' — a short poetic way to say the new day found those two men.
segments.s089-g15: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / out of', and گرد (here) means 'circle / ring / throng' (ز گرد سواران جهان = 'from the ring/throng of the world's horsemen'). تار شد is a poetic/archaic verbal phrase meaning 'was entangled / ensnared / overwhelmed' (تار + شد). The clause thus says that, surrounded by the ring of horsemen, he became trapped.
segments.s089-g16: سرانجام is the adverb 'in the end / finally'. نوذر is the subject name. گرفتار شد is a passive/periphrastic construction meaning 'was caught / was captured' (گرفتار = 'caught/ensnared', شد = auxiliary 'became/was'). Together: 'At last Nowzar was captured.'
segments.s089-g33: بجویید is an imperative (2nd person plural/polite) ‘search/seek’. تا introduces the purpose/result clause here (‘so that/until’). قارن is the proper name; رزم‌زن is a compound: رزم (battle) + زن (from زدن ‘to strike’, used here as ‘striker’ = ‘fighter’), so together ‘Qaran, the warrior’. Word order places the imperative main verb before the subordinate clause introduced by تا.
segments.s089-g34: رهایی نیابد is a negative potential/subjunctive idea: نیابد (subjunctive/grammatical form) with رهایی (‘freedom/release’) yields ‘may not find freedom / cannot escape’. از این انجمن (‘from this assembly’) uses poetic /ین spelling (این) after از to mark ‘from this’. Word order puts the thing not obtained (رهایی) before the verb نیابد.
segments.s089-g35: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when’. بشنید is simple past ‘he heard’. کاو here is an archaic/poetic element meaning ‘که او’ (‘that he’) and is not the literal verb ‘to search’; it introduces the reported clause. پیش ازان رفته بود (lit. ‘had gone before that’) is past perfect: رفته + بود (auxiliary) = ‘had gone’.
segments.s089-g36: ز کار شبستان is a prepositional/ablative phrase ‘from the work (کار) of the bedchamber (شبستان)’. برآشفته is a predicative adjective/participle ‘agitated/disturbed’, and بود is the past copula ‘was’. Together: ‘he was disturbed/roused from the bedchamber's work’. Note the poetic preposition ز for از.
segments.s089-g37: غمی گشت is an existential/impersonal turn: گشت ‘became’ with غمی ‘a grief’ yields ‘grief arose’. ازان کار (‘from that affair’) gives the cause/source. افراسیاب placed at the end is the person affected (typical Persian word order: predicate then agent/experiencer).
segments.s089-g38: ازو دور شد literally ‘he moved away from it’ (ازو ‘from it/him’ + دور شد ‘became distant/withdrew’). The following short clause/items خورد و آرام و خواب list his subsequent states/actions: خورد (past ‘he ate’), آرام (predicate ‘was calm’), خواب (sleep — understood ‘he slept’). Classical/Persian poetic style often strings such predicates without repeating verbs.
segments.s089-g39: که introduces the subordinate/temporal clause (‘that/when’). قارن رها یافت از وی = ‘Qaran found release / was freed from him’ — رها is ‘free/unbound’ and یافت (here ‘obtained’) makes the verbal sense. به جان links to the next clause by specifying the target/location (‘in the heart’/‘to the soul’) where the ensuing pain appears.
segments.s089-g40: بران (‘upon that’/‘thereon’) refers back to به جان — ‘upon that in his heart’. درد پیچید is a figurative verbal phrase: درد ‘pain’ + پیچید ‘twisted/gnawed’ = ‘a pain gripped/twisted (his heart)’. و شد بدگمان: شد ‘became’ + بدگمان ‘suspicious’ gives the resulting state ‘he became suspicious’. The sequence links cause (release) to emotion (pain) to mental state (suspicion).

sentences.s089-s1: When Nowzar heard that Qāren had gone, he set off after him in pursuit and hastened.
sentences.s089-s2: He kept charging on so that his unlucky day might pass, lest his heaven be handed underfoot.
sentences.s089-s3: When Afrāsiāb learned the news from him that he had turned his face toward the desert.
sentences.s089-s4: He mustered the army and the pursuers went forth; like a lion he turned to follow him and rushed.
sentences.s089-s13: That same one bestows the crown and a lofty throne; that same one bestows gloom and disgrace.
sentences.s089-s14: He is the same toward enemy and toward friend; sometimes you find his core, at other times only his skin.
sentences.s089-s15: If a black cloud settles on your head, in the end his place is the dust.
sentences.s089-s16: After that Afrasiab said (or commanded) that from cave and mountain and wilderness and water...
sentences.s089-s9: You said there is no place on the ground to drag him and the twelve hundred renowned men.
sentences.s089-s10: They tried many routes and fled, and also fell into the snare of calamity.
sentences.s089-s11: Having thus taken that army in bonds, he brought (them) before the high/lofty king.
sentences.s089-s12: If Fortune secretly sides with you, still you will not find justification in its turning.
sentences.s089-s21: Thus he said to the famed Viseh: "Harden your heart with regard to the son's death."
sentences.s089-s22: When Qaran brought Kaveh into battle, the panther paused because of his haste.
sentences.s089-s23: You must go; gird your waist and make (raise) a skilled army.
sentences.s089-s5: When distress came upon the king, he saw nothing but repeated charges and the battle.
sentences.s089-s6: In such a manner he came, springing along the way, so that he might bring a head un‑helmeted to (someone's) head.
sentences.s089-s7: The dark night lasted until the sun rose high; the day then came upon Nowzar and Afrasiab.
sentences.s089-s8: From the ring of the world's horsemen he became ensnared; at last Nowzar was taken captive.
sentences.s089-s17: Search until Qaran the warrior cannot get free from this assembly.
sentences.s089-s18: When he heard that he had gone off before that, he was roused from the bedchamber's work.
sentences.s089-s19: From that affair grief came upon Afrasiab; he withdrew from it, ate, became calm, and slept.
sentences.s089-s20: When Qaran obtained his release from him, a pain twisted in his heart and he became suspicious.
